properties.name: amp
properties.description: PyTorch Automatic Mixed Precision (torch.amp)
properties.example_code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
device = 'cuda' if torch.cuda.is_available() else 'cpu'
model = nn.Linear(64, 32).to(device)
opt = torch.optim.SGD(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
x = torch.randn(8, 64, device=device)
y = torch.randn(8, 32, device=device)
opt.zero_grad(set_to_none=True)
with torch.autocast(device_type='cuda' if device == 'cuda' else 'cpu', dtype=torch.bfloat16):
    out = model(x)
    loss = (out - y).pow(2).mean()
loss.backward()
opt.step()

properties.description: CUDA C++ Programming Guide -- bfloat16 floating-point format
properties.example_code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
device = 'cuda' if torch.cuda.is_available() else 'cpu'
model = nn.Linear(64, 32).to(device)
opt = torch.optim.SGD(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
x = torch.randn(8, 64, device=device)
y = torch.randn(8, 32, device=device)
opt.zero_grad(set_to_none=True)
with torch.autocast(device_type='cuda' if device == 'cuda' else 'cpu', dtype=torch.bfloat16):
    out = model(x)
    loss = (out - y).pow(2).mean()
loss.backward()
opt.step()

properties.name: cuda_graphs
properties.description:
CUDA Graphs capture a sequence of kernel launches and their
dependencies into a single replayable object; the overhead of
graph definition and instantiation can then be amortized over many
executions. A graph is a snapshot of a workflow including kernels,
their parameters, and their dependencies, replayed as rapidly as
possible. Best for workloads with many small kernels and fixed
launch parameters; structures with variable shapes or per-step
control flow cannot be captured directly. See the CUDA C++
Programming Guide CUDA Graphs section for the full set of capture
restrictions and the API surface.
properties.example_code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
if not torch.cuda.is_available():
    raise SystemExit('SKIP: no CUDA')
model = nn.Linear(64, 32).cuda()
static_x = torch.randn(8, 64, device='cuda')
for _ in range(3):
    out = model(static_x)
torch.cuda.synchronize()
g = torch.cuda.CUDAGraph()
with torch.cuda.graph(g):
    out = model(static_x)
g.replay()
torch.cuda.synchronize()

properties.name: fp16
properties.description: CUDA C++ Programming Guide -- half-precision (FP16) floating-point format
properties.example_code:
import torch
cc = torch.cuda.get_device_capability()
generation = {
    (7, 0): '1gen (Volta)',     (7, 5): '2gen (Turing)',
    (8, 0): '3gen (Ampere DC)', (8, 6): '3gen (Ampere)',
    (8, 9): '4gen (Ada)',       (9, 0): '4gen (Hopper)',
    (10, 0): '5gen (Blackwell DC)', (12, 0): '5gen (Blackwell GeForce)',
}.get(cc, f'unknown (CC {cc[0]}.{cc[1]})')
print(f'GPU CC={cc[0]}.{cc[1]}, tensor core {generation}')
print(f'compiled archs: {torch.cuda.get_arch_list()}')

properties.name: fp4
properties.description: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 5090 (FP4 + 5th-gen Tensor Cores)
properties.example_code:
import torch
cc = torch.cuda.get_device_capability()
# FP4 tensor cores live on sm_100 (Blackwell DC) and sm_120 (Blackwell GeForce).
if cc < (10, 0):
    raise SystemExit(
        f'fp4 unsupported on CC {cc[0]}.{cc[1]} '
        '(requires Blackwell sm_100 or sm_120)'
    )
# Inference-only example. Real call sites use NVIDIA Transformer Engine
# or TensorRT-LLM with an MXFP4 recipe. PyTorch native fp4 autocast
# does not exist (as of torch 2.12); MXFP4 is reached via te.fp8_autocast
# with a Format.MXFP4 recipe in recent transformer_engine releases.
print(f'GPU CC={cc[0]}.{cc[1]} supports MXFP4 inference via Transformer Engine')

properties.name: fp8
properties.description: FP8 Formats for Deep Learning (Micikevicius et al., 2022)
properties.example_code:
import torch
if not torch.cuda.is_available():
    raise SystemExit('SKIP: no CUDA')
cc = torch.cuda.get_device_capability()
# FP8 Tensor Cores require compute capability >= 8.9 (Ada / Hopper / Blackwell)
if cc < (8, 9):
    raise SystemExit(
        f'SKIP: fp8 requires CC >= 8.9, got {cc[0]}.{cc[1]}'
    )
try:
    import transformer_engine.pytorch as te
    from transformer_engine.common.recipe import DelayedScaling, Format
except ImportError:
    raise SystemExit('SKIP: transformer_engine not installed')
recipe = DelayedScaling(
    margin=0,
    fp8_format=Format.HYBRID,    # forward=E4M3, backward=E5M2
    amax_history_len=16,
    amax_compute_algo='max',
)
te_model = te.Linear(64, 32).cuda()
x = torch.randn(8, 64, device='cuda')
with te.fp8_autocast(enabled=True, fp8_recipe=recipe):
    out = te_model(x)

properties.name: int8
properties.description: PyTorch Quantization (INT8 quantized inference)
properties.example_code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from torch.ao.quantization import quantize_dynamic
fp32_model = nn.Sequential(nn.Linear(128, 256), nn.ReLU(), nn.Linear(256, 10))
int8_model = quantize_dynamic(fp32_model, {nn.Linear}, dtype=torch.qint8)
x = torch.randn(4, 128)
out = int8_model(x)

properties.name: tensor_cores
properties.description:
Set torch.backends.cudnn.benchmark=True so that when a cuDNN
convolution is called with a new set of size parameters, cuDNN runs
multiple algorithms, benchmarks them to find the fastest one, and
reuses it for the rest of the process for the same size parameters.
Disabling it (benchmark=False) makes cuDNN deterministically select
an algorithm, possibly at the cost of reduced performance. The
separate torch.backends.cudnn.deterministic flag controls
reproducibility and is distinct from the benchmark setting; combine
it with seeded RNGs (Python, NumPy, PyTorch) when you need
reproducible runs.
properties.example_code:
import torch
cc = torch.cuda.get_device_capability()
generation = {
    (7, 0): '1gen (Volta)',     (7, 5): '2gen (Turing)',
    (8, 0): '3gen (Ampere DC)', (8, 6): '3gen (Ampere)',
    (8, 9): '4gen (Ada)',       (9, 0): '4gen (Hopper)',
    (10, 0): '5gen (Blackwell DC)', (12, 0): '5gen (Blackwell GeForce)',
}.get(cc, f'unknown (CC {cc[0]}.{cc[1]})')
print(f'GPU CC={cc[0]}.{cc[1]}, tensor core {generation}')
print(f'compiled archs: {torch.cuda.get_arch_list()}')

properties.name: NVIDIA B100
recommended_patterns: use PyTorch SDPA before hard-coded flash-attn dependency [https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/2.12/generated/torch.nn.functional.scaled_dot_product_attention.html]
activation checkpointing when activation memory dominates [https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/2.12/checkpoint.html]
sequence packing and KV-cache budget before longer context
treat B100 as provisional until runtime and product-sheet validation [https://docs.nvidia.com/datacenter/dcgm/latest/user-guide/dcgm-diagnostics.html]
confirm device name, memory, and compute capability with nvidia-smi/torch before sizing
use B200/GB200 patterns only after runtime device reports B200 or GB200
avoid_patterns: no production sizing from B100 metadata without official SKU sheet
no B200/GB200 memory, MIG, or sm_100 assumptions from B100 name alone
no FP4/MXFP8 default without kernel and accuracy validation
no CUDA extension target without runtime-verified compute capability
properties.official_spec_status: partial: NVIDIA DCGM lists B100 as NVBandwidth-supported, but current public NVIDIA MIG/CUDA tables do not publish B100 memory, compute capability, or MIG profile data

properties.name: cut_cross_entropy
properties.description: Cut Cross-Entropy: fuses a linear classifier/logit projection with the cross-entropy loss so the (N x V) logits tensor is never materialized in global memory [https://github.com/apple/ml-cross-entropy]; chunked online log-sum-exp in SRAM. Same math (exact CE + gradient), >4x peak-memory cut on large-vocab heads, >2x kernel speed.
properties.example_code:
import torch
from cut_cross_entropy import linear_cross_entropy

B, T, D, V = 2, 16, 1024, 32000
embeddings = torch.randn(B, T, D, device='cuda', dtype=torch.bfloat16, requires_grad=True)
classifier = torch.randn(V, D, device='cuda', dtype=torch.bfloat16, requires_grad=True)
labels = torch.randint(0, V, (B, T), device='cuda')
loss = linear_cross_entropy(embeddings, classifier, labels, shift=1)
loss.backward()

properties.name: muon_optimizer
properties.description: Muon optimizer: for 2D weight matrices, replaces the momentum-smoothed gradient with its nearest semi-orthogonal matrix via a fixed 5-step Newton-Schulz iteration (no SVD); 1D params stay on AdamW. Matches AdamW final loss using ~52% of training FLOPs.
properties.tensor_core_generation: not_required; NS/GEMM-heavy optimizer step can benefit from fast matmul
properties.example_code:
import torch

muon_params = []
adamw_params = []
for name, p in model.named_parameters():
    if not p.requires_grad:
        continue
    is_hidden_2d = p.ndim == 2 and 'embed' not in name and 'lm_head' not in name
    if is_hidden_2d:
        muon_params.append(p)
    else:
        adamw_params.append(p)

optim_muon = torch.optim.Muon(muon_params, lr=0.02, weight_decay=0.1, momentum=0.95, nesterov=True, ns_steps=5, adjust_lr_fn='match_rms_adamw')
optim_adamw = torch.optim.AdamW(adamw_params, lr=3e-4, weight_decay=0.01)

optim_muon.zero_grad()
optim_adamw.zero_grad()
loss.backward()
optim_muon.step()
optim_adamw.step()
properties.usage: Hybrid Muon+AdamW training optimizer for matmul-heavy candidates: the example code shows the parameter split, Muon settings, and AdamW companion optimizer.
properties.limitations: software training optimizer; requires a PyTorch build that exposes torch.optim.Muon; no hardware-specific acceleration is required; use only on selected 2D hidden weights and keep AdamW for embeddings/bias/norm/head/non-2D params; benchmark against tuned AdamW; not an inference feature
recommended_patterns: hybrid Muon+AdamW for matmul-heavy training with a tuned AdamW baseline
split params by dimensionality/name: Muon for hidden 2D matrices, AdamW for embeddings, heads, bias, norm, and non-2D params
tune Muon lr/momentum/ns_steps separately from AdamW lr/weight_decay
avoid_patterns: no Muon on embedding/bias/norm/head/non-2D params and no comparison against undertuned AdamW
no blind AdamW replacement in small CNN, tabular, inference, or memory-bound runs
properties.notes: Use Muon on selected 2D hidden weights and AdamW on embedding/bias/norm/head/non-2D params; tune Muon and AdamW learning rates separately; tune weight_decay per optimizer group; prefer adjust_lr_fn='match_rms_adamw' when reusing an AdamW LR schedule; compare against a tuned AdamW baseline.

properties.name: soap_optimizer
properties.description: SOAP optimizer: Shampoo run in the eigenbasis of its own preconditioner, then plain Adam in that rotated space; amortizes eigendecomposition. >40% fewer iterations / >35% wall-clock vs AdamW to same loss in large-batch regime.
properties.tensor_core_generation: not_required; preconditioner math is matrix-operation heavy
properties.example_code:
import torch
from torch import nn
from soap import SOAP

model = nn.Linear(16, 4)
x = torch.randn(8, 16)
loss = model(x).pow(2).mean()
optim = SOAP(model.parameters(), lr=3e-3, betas=(0.95, 0.95), weight_decay=0.01, precondition_frequency=10)
optim.zero_grad()
loss.backward()
optim.step()
properties.usage: Software optimizer feature for large-batch or optimizer-limited experiments after a tuned AdamW baseline; no hardware-specific SOAP pattern is defined in this file.
properties.limitations: preconditioner and eigendecomposition overhead plus optimizer state can outweigh benefits on small, latency-bound, or memory-constrained runs; benchmark against tuned AdamW before adoption
recommended_patterns: try SOAP only after a tuned AdamW baseline in large-batch or optimizer-limited training
track optimizer-step time, peak memory, and validation loss because preconditioner cost can dominate
avoid_patterns: no SOAP as a universal default for small, latency-bound, or memory-constrained runs
no adoption without verifying the third-party SOAP package/API in the runtime
properties.notes: LLM-facing rule: classify SOAP as an experimental software optimizer candidate, not a hardware feature. Use when optimizer convergence is the bottleneck; skip when data/model execution dominates.

properties.name: ademamix_optimizer
properties.description: AdEMAMix optimizer: augments Adam's fast gradient EMA with a second very-slow EMA (beta3 ~0.9999). A 1.3B model on 101B tokens matches AdamW on 197B (~51% token reduction to same loss). One extra EMA buffer.
properties.tensor_core_generation: not_required; Adam-style optimizer state update is software-side
properties.example_code:
import torch
from torch import nn
from ademamix import AdEMAMix

model = nn.Linear(16, 4)
x = torch.randn(8, 16)
loss = model(x).pow(2).mean()
num_iterations = 256000
optim = AdEMAMix(params=model.parameters(), lr=1e-3, betas=(0.9, 0.999, 0.9999), alpha=8.0, beta3_warmup=num_iterations, alpha_warmup=num_iterations, weight_decay=0.1)
optim.zero_grad()
loss.backward()
optim.step()
properties.usage: Software optimizer feature that augments Adam-style EMAs with a slow EMA; no hardware-specific AdEMAMix pattern is defined in this file.
properties.limitations: adds optimizer state and requires alpha, beta3, alpha_warmup, beta3_warmup, learning-rate, and weight-decay tuning; benchmark against tuned AdamW before adoption
recommended_patterns: candidate for long-token or LLM-style training where AdamW token efficiency is the bottleneck
budget one extra EMA buffer and tune alpha/beta3 warmups with the LR schedule
avoid_patterns: no AdEMAMix as a drop-in default for short, small-data, or memory-constrained runs
no comparison against untuned AdamW or mismatched token/step budgets
properties.notes: LLM-facing rule: recommend only as an optimizer experiment with memory budget and tuned baseline; it gives no inference speedup and no hardware-specific acceleration.

properties.name: dataset_decomposition
properties.description: Dataset Decomposition: decompose corpus into same-length buckets and train a short-to-long variable-sequence-length curriculum with only intra-document attention. Changes data ordering not model/loss; reaches target accuracy up to 6x faster than fixed-length concatenation.

properties.name: gram_newton_schulz_symmetric_gemm
properties.description: Gram Newton-Schulz symmetric/triangular fused GEMM: the Newton-Schulz step (used by Muon) is a symmetric product; a triangular fused kernel removes the wasted half of multiplies plus a DRAM round-trip cuBLAS incurs. Up to 2x faster NS step. Same math (Gram-matrix restart guards stability).
properties.example_code:
import torch

# Conceptual fallback only. Production use should call the fused symmetric/triangular GEMM kernel.
def gram_newton_schulz_reference(X, ns_steps=5, eps=1e-7):
    X = X / (X.norm() + eps)
    wide = X.shape[0] < X.shape[1]
    for _ in range(ns_steps):
        if wide:
            G = X @ X.T
            X = 1.5 * X - 0.5 * (G @ X)
        else:
            G = X.T @ X
            X = 1.5 * X - 0.5 * (X @ G)
    return X

momentum_matrix = torch.randn(128, 64)
update = gram_newton_schulz_reference(momentum_matrix)
properties.usage: Kernel-level optimization for Muon/Newton-Schulz orthogonalization when profiler evidence shows the NS step is material. The example is a correctness fallback, not the fused production kernel.
properties.limitations: experimental custom-kernel idea; not a generic GEMM replacement; relevant only when Muon/Newton-Schulz optimizer-step time is significant; requires custom CUDA/Triton/CUTLASS implementation and numerical parity tests; source is a technical blog, not official vendor documentation
recommended_patterns: profile Muon optimizer-step time before considering the fused symmetric/triangular GEMM kernel
keep the reference Newton-Schulz implementation for correctness checks and fallback
avoid_patterns: no use without Muon/Newton-Schulz workload
no marking verified=true until benchmark and numerical parity are recorded
properties.notes: LLM-facing rule: suggest this only after Muon has been selected and profiler evidence shows orthogonalization dominates; otherwise prefer normal optimizer tuning.

properties.name: nvimagecodec_gpu_decode
properties.description: nvImageCodec GPU image decode: decodes encoded image bytes (nvJPEG/nvJPEG2000/nvTIFF plus hardware JPEG decode on documented nvJPEG hardware [https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/nvjpeg/index.html]) directly into GPU memory, DLPack zero-copy to torch, decode of batch N+1 overlapping compute of batch N. Removes CPU-decode bottleneck for CV/imaging. ~2.18x faster JPEG loading. No-op for text/LLM training.
properties.example_code:
import torch
from nvidia import nvimgcodec

decoder = nvimgcodec.Decoder()
nv_img = decoder.read('sample.jp2')
cap = nv_img.to_dlpack()
torch_img = torch.from_dlpack(cap)
assert torch_img.is_cuda
batch = torch_img.permute(2, 0, 1).unsqueeze(0).contiguous()

properties.name: async_tensor_parallel
properties.description: Asynchronous tensor-parallel communication overlap (PyTorch SymmetricMemory): uses eligible NCCL Copy Engine collectives such as all_gather_into_tensor/all_to_all_single on symmetric-memory tensors so communication can overlap compute. Multi-GPU only; no effect single-GPU. Same math.
properties.example_code:
import os
import torch
import torch.distributed as dist
import torch.distributed._symmetric_memory as symm_mem

rank = int(os.environ.get('LOCAL_RANK', '0'))
device = torch.device('cuda', rank)
opts = dist.ProcessGroupNCCL.Options()
opts.config.cta_policy = dist.ProcessGroupNCCL.NCCL_CTA_POLICY_ZERO
dist.init_process_group(backend='nccl', pg_options=opts, device_id=device)
world_size = dist.get_world_size()

symm_mem.set_backend('NCCL')
group_name = dist.group.WORLD.group_name
inp = symm_mem.empty(1024 * 1024, device=device)
out = symm_mem.empty(1024 * 1024 * world_size, device=device)
symm_mem.rendezvous(inp, group=group_name)
symm_mem.rendezvous(out, group=group_name)
work = dist.all_gather_into_tensor(out, inp, async_op=True)
# compute can be scheduled here on independent work
work.wait()

properties.name: fp8_rowwise_scaling
properties.description: FP8 row-wise (per-token) scaling: one fp8 scale factor per row of each operand instead of per-tensor; a single outlier row no longer forces a coarse scale that underflows the rest. Strictly more accuracy-preserving than per-tensor fp8; loss parity with bf16. GEMMs still on fp8 Tensor Cores (~1.11x over bf16 H100). Available via torchao.
properties.example_code:
import torch
from torch import nn
from torchao.float8 import Float8LinearConfig, Float8LinearRecipeName, convert_to_float8_training

model = nn.Sequential(nn.Linear(2048, 4096), nn.GELU(), nn.Linear(4096, 2048)).bfloat16().cuda()
config = Float8LinearConfig.from_recipe_name(Float8LinearRecipeName.ROWWISE)
convert_to_float8_training(model, config=config)
model = torch.compile(model)
x = torch.randn(16, 2048, device='cuda', dtype=torch.bfloat16)
y = model(x)

properties.name: fp8_all_gather_fsdp2
properties.description: FP8 all-gather in FSDP2: shards and all-gathers parameters in fp8 instead of bf16, halving the sharded-parameter communication volume in distributed training. Multi-GPU only. Built on torchao float8 + FSDP2.
properties.example_code:
import torch
from torch import nn
from torchao.float8 import Float8LinearConfig, convert_to_float8_training
from torchao.float8 import precompute_float8_dynamic_scale_for_fsdp
from torch.distributed.fsdp import fully_shard

model = nn.Sequential(nn.Linear(4096, 4096), nn.GELU(), nn.Linear(4096, 4096)).bfloat16().cuda()
config = Float8LinearConfig(enable_fsdp_float8_all_gather=True)
convert_to_float8_training(model, config=config)
for layer in model:
    if isinstance(layer, nn.Linear):
        fully_shard(layer)
fully_shard(model)
# In the distributed training loop, call after optimizer.step():
precompute_float8_dynamic_scale_for_fsdp(model)
